corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: After all, unsupervised learning cannot, by definition, know what will be useful for the task at hand.

Future work may explore replacing the small MDN-RNN network with higher capacity models , or incorporating an external memory module , if we want our agent to learn to explore more complicated worlds.
Experiments with those more general approaches are left for future work.
The figure below is a VAE model trained on screenshots from CarRacing-v0.
This is not what we do here though -our present approach is still closer to some of the older systems , where a RNN M is used to predict and plan ahead step by step.
Nevertheless, at least in our present C-M variant, M's predictions are essential for teaching C, more like in some of the early C-M systems , but combined with evolution or black box optimization.
We present a simple model inspired by our own cognitive system.

In many reinforcement learning (RL) problems , an artificial agent also benefits from having a good representation of past and present states, and a good predictive model of the future , preferably a powerful predictive model implemented on a general purpose computer such as a recurrent neural network (RNN) .

The RL algorithm is often bottlenecked by the credit assignment problem, which makes it hard for traditional RL algorithms to learn millions of weights of a large model, hence in practice, smaller networks are used as they iterate faster to a good policy during training.
The backpropagation algorithm can be used to train large neural networks efficiently.
A small controller lets the training algorithm focus on the credit assignment problem on a small search space, while not sacrificing capacity and expressiveness via the larger world model.

In this article, we present a simplified framework that we can use to experimentally demonstrate some of the key concepts from these papers, and also suggest further insights to effectively apply these ideas to various RL environments.

While Gaussian processes work well with a small set of low dimension data, their computational complexity makes them difficult to scale up to model a large history of high dimensional observations.
To train our V model, we first collect a dataset of 10,000 random rollouts of the environment.
We use this dataset to train V to learn a latent space of each frame observed.

Our agent is able to achieve a score of 906 ± 21 over 100 random trials, effectively solving the task and obtaining new state of the art results.

The benefit of implementing the world model as a fully differentiable recurrent computation graph also means that we may be able to train our agents in the dream directly using the backpropagation algorithm to fine-tune its policy to maximize an objective function .

Deploying all of these machine learning models in a web browser was made possible with deeplearn.js, a hardware-accelerated machine learning framework for the browser, developed by the People+AI Research Initiative (PAIR) team at Google.
